db_indicators: Supabase dir: ./supabase (migrations, schemas, functions, seed files)
DB env var in .env.example: VITE_SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_PROJECT_REF, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, SUPABASE_DB_PASSWORD

checks.name: Schema source of truth exists
checks.evidence: 6 declarative schema files in supabase/schemas/: tables-core.sql (261 lines), tables-junctions.sql (159 lines), tables-notes.sql (66 lines), tables-reference.sql (88 lines), views.sql (321 lines), functions.sql (274 lines). Total: 1,169 lines of schema-as-code.
checks.details: The project uses Supabase declarative schemas. An AI coding agent can read supabase/schemas/ and understand the full database schema including all tables, columns, relationships, views, and stored functions without needing to reconstruct from migration history.
checks.remediation: None required. Schema source of truth is excellent.

checks.name: Generated types or typed models exist
checks.evidence: src/types/database.generated.ts exists (5,919 lines). STALE: generated file mtime is 44,352s (~12.3 hours) older than latest migration 20260303120000_fix_admin_restore_sale_metadata.sql. git status shows src/types/database.types.ts as deleted (recent rename or restructure).
checks.details: Generated types are comprehensive but stale. An AI agent reading these types would miss fields added in the final migration. Score is partial (1/2) due to staleness.
checks.remediation: Run npx supabase gen types typescript --project-id <ref> > src/types/database.generated.ts after each migration. Consider adding to CI or a post-migration script.

checks.name: Migration history present and sequential
checks.evidence: 17 migration files in supabase/migrations/ using timestamped naming (YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_description.sql). Oldest: 20260214003329_remote_schema.sql. Newest: 20260303120000_fix_admin_restore_sale_metadata.sql. No gaps detected across 17 days of schema evolution.
checks.details: Migration files follow strict sequential timestamp convention with descriptive names. History is complete and traceable. An AI agent can reconstruct full schema evolution and understand the rationale for each change.
checks.remediation: None required. Migration history is well-structured and sequential.

checks.name: Data access layer identifiable
checks.evidence: 32 files in src/atomic-crm/providers/supabase/handlers/ (activitiesHandler.ts, contactsHandler.ts, opportunitiesHandler.ts, organizationsHandler.ts, etc.). All routed through src/atomic-crm/providers/supabase/composedDataProvider.ts. CLAUDE.md explicitly enforces this pattern. Supabase client used in only 10 files, all within provider boundary.
checks.details: Exemplary data access architecture. Every resource has a dedicated handler file registered through composedDataProvider.ts. No scattered direct DB calls in feature components. An AI agent can immediately locate where any database query happens.
checks.remediation: None required. Data access layer is clearly structured and enforced.

checks.name: Database MCP server configured
checks.evidence: .mcp.json configures Supabase MCP at https://mcp.supabase.com/mcp. .claude/settings.json allows: mcp__supabase__execute_sql, mcp__supabase__list_tables, mcp__supabase__apply_migration, mcp__supabase__get_logs, mcp__supabase__generate_typescript_types.
checks.details: Supabase MCP server is fully configured with fine-grained permissions. An AI agent can query live schema, execute SQL, inspect tables, apply migrations, and regenerate types via MCP protocol.
checks.remediation: None required. Database MCP is fully configured with appropriate permissions.

checks.name: Seed data or fixtures exist
checks.evidence: supabase/seed.sql, supabase/seed-e2e.sql, supabase/seed-complete-flow.sql, supabase/seed-sample-opportunity.sql. CSV fixtures: tests/fixtures/contacts-valid.csv, contacts-invalid.csv, contacts-formula-injection.csv. Data files: data/production-data/seed_contacts.csv, seed_organizations.csv. TypeScript fixtures in handlers/__tests__/fixtures/opportunities.ts. npm scripts: seed:e2e:dashboard-v3, generate:seed.
checks.details: Exceptionally rich seed data setup covering SQL seeds for multiple scenarios, CSV fixtures for import testing, TypeScript fixtures for unit testing, and npm convenience scripts.
checks.remediation: None required. Seed data coverage is comprehensive.

checks.name: Schema documented in AI context files
checks.evidence: CLAUDE.md has dedicated Testing and Database section with 27 total DB mentions (Postgres 17, RLS 100%, soft deletes via deleted_at, edge functions). .claude/rules/DATABASE_LAYER.md provides 13 DB rules (DB-001 through DB-013). 14 rule files total reference database concepts. Schema-specific references in .claude/skills/engineering/supabase-postgres-best-practices/references/.
checks.details: Database documentation in AI context files is excellent. CLAUDE.md documents the DB stack, security model, soft-delete contract, and dev workflow. DATABASE_LAYER.md provides detailed rules for modifying the DB layer.
checks.remediation: None required. Consider adding a brief table inventory to CLAUDE.md listing entity tables and their business purpose.

checks.name: Database connection patterns documented
checks.evidence: .env.example contains 14 database-related variables: VITE_SUPABASE_URL, VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, SUPABASE_PROJECT_REF, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, SUPABASE_DB_PASSWORD, with comments explaining CLI vs runtime vs edge function usage. README.md mentions connection patterns. CLAUDE.md documents cloud-only dev workflow.
checks.details: .env.example is thorough with all required credentials documented and annotated. An AI agent can immediately identify all required connection credentials and understand when each is used.
checks.remediation: None required. Connection patterns are well-documented.
